content.text: Distributed tracing for AI agents is the practice of propagating a shared trace context across every component that participates in an agent's reasoning loop — the agent runtime, LLM API calls, tool executions, sub-agent invocations, and retrieval systems — so that a single end-to-end trace records exactly what happened, in what order, and how long each step took.
is the complete story of one user request from arrival to final response. It is built from

— individually timed units of work. In an agentic system the root span represents the user request; beneath it you find the agent reasoning span, which nests LLM API call spans, tool execution spans, retrieval spans, and — when the agent spawns sub-agents — entire sub-trees of child spans that share the same
The standard wire format for carrying this context across process and service boundaries is the

HTTP headers). When an agent calls an external tool over HTTP, it injects these headers; the tool receives them and creates its own child spans with the correct parent, producing one connected graph rather than isolated fragments.
(OTel) — a vendor-neutral SDK that handles span creation, attribute attachment, context propagation, and export over OTLP (the OTel Protocol) to any compatible backend.

Debugging a failed agentic task without distributed tracing is an exercise in reading disconnected log lines and guessing. The agent may have looped five times before giving up; you see only the final error. With a trace you see each loop iteration as a separate span with its own duration and attributes, the exact prompt and model used in each LLM call, which tool calls succeeded or failed, and how long retrieval took.

The stakes are higher for agents than for ordinary microservices because agents can take non-deterministic, variable-depth execution paths. A microservice either calls its downstream dependency or it does not. An agent might call the LLM once or twenty times depending on intermediate results, spawn zero or three sub-agents, and invoke tools in an order that was not written in any code. Every deviation from the expected path is a debugging event — and without trace data, you have no systematic way to find it.

Cost visibility is an equally important driver. LLM API calls are priced per token, and an agent loop that runs longer than expected can generate thousands of tokens in minutes. Tracing every LLM span with its token counts and model name allows you to identify runaway loops before they become runaway bills.
One trace ID links every span from user request to final answer, even across process and service boundaries.
When a task fails, the failing span is directly visible with its attributes — no log-grepping required.
Waterfall views in Jaeger or Honeycomb reveal which spans dominate total duration — LLM latency, tool API latency, or agent orchestration overhead.
to every LLM span enables per-task and per-agent token accounting.
attribute on each agent iteration span makes it trivial to spot tasks with unexpectedly high iteration counts.
Comparing trace profiles across deployments reveals latency or token regressions before users report them.
OTel instrumentation runs identically against Jaeger, Honeycomb, Datadog, Grafana Tempo, or any OTLP-compatible backend — no lock-in.
Why Agent Tracing Is Harder Than Microservice Tracing

A trace from a web framework always has roughly the same shape. An agent trace may be two spans deep or fifty spans deep depending on the task. Sampling strategies designed for uniform depth break down.
An agent reasoning loop can create thousands of spans in a single trace. Naive head-based sampling at a fixed rate will miss most of an interesting long-running trace.
Tools invoked via async HTTP or queued execution create context propagation gaps if the developer forgets to attach the
header or thread the OTel context through the async boundary.
When an agent spawns a sub-agent in a separate process or microservice, the parent trace context must be serialized and passed as part of the sub-agent invocation payload, not just HTTP headers.
Attaching full prompt and completion text to spans may violate data governance rules. Attributes must be selectively redacted or hashed.
Long-running agents produce large traces. Uncontrolled trace volume can make backends expensive and slow.
Every OTel span carries four identifiers: a 128-bit
shared by all spans in one request, a 64-bit
pointing at the calling span, and a
byte that indicates whether this trace is being sampled.
For an agentic task the hierarchy looks like this:
— created at the API gateway or agent entry point. Attributes:
— one span per reasoning loop iteration. Attributes:
— one per vector database query. Attributes:
— a child span that becomes the root of an entirely new sub-tree. The
is the same; the sub-agent's spans nest under the parent's sub-agent span.
header encodes four fields: version, trace-id, parent-id, and trace-flags, separated by hyphens:
When the agent runtime calls a tool over HTTP, it injects this header. When the tool microservice receives the call, it extracts the header and starts its own child span using the received
For in-process async code, OTel context must be explicitly attached to async tasks or thread pools because Python's
tasks do not automatically inherit the parent context variable.

attrs.alt: Distributed Tracing — Agent Request Trace Flow diagram showing span hierarchy from User Request through Agent Runtime to LLM API, Tool Execution, Sub-Agent invocations and a Trace Backend pipeline

content.text: The diagram shows the full span hierarchy. The OTel Collector is a sidecar or standalone process that receives OTLP-formatted spans from the agent runtime over gRPC or HTTP, batches them, and fans them out to one or more backends such as Jaeger (open-source), Honeycomb, or Datadog. The collector is the recommended deployment pattern because it decouples instrumentation from backend choice — swapping from Jaeger to Honeycomb requires only a collector configuration change, not a code change.
Any agentic system that calls external tools or services — even a single HTTP tool call creates a cross-process boundary that benefits from trace context propagation.
Multi-agent pipelines where orchestrator and workers are separate processes — without tracing you cannot reconstruct which sub-agent was responsible for a failure.
Production systems where debugging from logs alone is too slow or too expensive.
Agentic tasks that run for more than a few seconds — latency attribution requires tracing.
Teams that need to charge LLM costs back to users, departments, or projects — token counts per span enable this.

Distributed tracing is overkill for: short-lived scripts that run offline and never involve external services; quick research agents where a flat log is sufficient; or prototype code that will be discarded.
Install the OTel Python SDK and OTLP exporter:
Bootstrap the tracer once at application startup:

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.resources import Resource

# Identify this service in every span
resource = Resource.create({
    "service.name": "agentic-research-agent",
    "service.version": "1.3.0",
    "deployment.environment": "production",
})

# Send spans to the OTel Collector over gRPC
exporter = OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint="http://otel-collector:4317", insecure=True)
provider = TracerProvider(resource=resource)
provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(exporter))
trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)

tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

import anthropic
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.trace import StatusCode

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
tracer = trace.get_tracer("agent.reasoning")

def run_agent_task(task_id: str, user_input: str) -> str:
    """Run the full agent loop, emitting one span per step."""
    with tracer.start_as_current_span(
        "agent.task",
        attributes={
            "agent.task_id": task_id,
            "agent.task_type": "research",
        },
    ) as task_span:
        messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_input}]
        tools = get_tool_definitions()  # your tool schema list
        step = 0

        while True:
            step += 1
            with tracer.start_as_current_span(
                "agent.step",
                attributes={"agent.step_number": step, "agent.task_id": task_id},
            ) as step_span:
                try:
                    response = call_llm_with_tracing(messages, tools)
                except Exception as exc:
                    step_span.set_status(StatusCode.ERROR, str(exc))
                    task_span.set_status(StatusCode.ERROR, str(exc))
                    raise

                if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
                    return extract_text(response)

                tool_results = execute_tools_with_tracing(response.content)
                messages = append_tool_results(messages, response.content, tool_results)


def call_llm_with_tracing(messages, tools):
    """Wrap a single LLM API call in its own span."""
    with tracer.start_as_current_span(
        "llm.call",
        attributes={"llm.model": "claude-opus-4-5", "llm.temperature": 0.0},
    ) as span:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=4096,
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages,
        )
        # Attach token counts so cost can be computed later
        span.set_attribute("llm.input_tokens", response.usage.input_tokens)
        span.set_attribute("llm.output_tokens", response.usage.output_tokens)
        span.set_attribute("llm.stop_reason", response.stop_reason)
        return response


def execute_tools_with_tracing(content_blocks):
    """Execute each tool call in its own child span."""
    results = []
    for block in content_blocks:
        if block.type != "tool_use":
            continue
        with tracer.start_as_current_span(
            "tool.call",
            attributes={
                "tool.name": block.name,
                "tool.use_id": block.id,
            },
        ) as span:
            try:
                result = dispatch_tool(block.name, block.input)
                span.set_attribute("tool.status", "success")
                results.append({"tool_use_id": block.id, "content": result})
            except Exception as exc:
                span.set_status(StatusCode.ERROR, str(exc))
                span.set_attribute("tool.status", "error")
                results.append({"tool_use_id": block.id, "content": f"Error: {exc}"})
    return results
Propagating Context into HTTP Tool Calls

import httpx
from opentelemetry.propagate import inject

def call_external_tool_api(endpoint: str, payload: dict) -> dict:
    """Inject W3C traceparent header so the downstream service can create child spans."""
    headers = {}
    inject(headers)  # populates 'traceparent' and 'tracestate'
    response = httpx.post(endpoint, json=payload, headers=headers)
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()

# Alternatively, use opentelemetry-instrumentation-httpx for auto-propagation:
# from opentelemetry.instrumentation.httpx import HTTPXClientInstrumentation
# HTTPXClientInstrumentation().instrument()
Span events are timestamped log entries attached to a span. They are ideal for recording agent reasoning steps without creating a full child span:

from opentelemetry import trace

def record_reasoning_step(span, step_description: str, tool_chosen: str | None = None):
    """Add a timestamped event to the current agent step span."""
    attributes = {"reasoning.description": step_description[:512]}
    if tool_chosen:
        attributes["reasoning.tool_chosen"] = tool_chosen
    span.add_event("agent.reasoning_step", attributes=attributes)

# Inside the agent loop:
with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent.step") as span:
    record_reasoning_step(span, "Decided to call web_search tool", tool_chosen="web_search")
    record_reasoning_step(span, "Search returned 5 results, picking top 2")
    record_reasoning_step(span, "Synthesizing final answer from retrieved context")

A financial research agent at a fintech company was intermittently returning incorrect analysis. Logs showed only the final answer. After adding distributed tracing, engineers loaded the failing trace in Honeycomb and immediately saw: in one run the agent had looped 18 times instead of the normal 3–5. The LLM call spans in iterations 6–18 showed
on every iteration, but the tool call spans for those iterations showed
with a rate-limit message from the data provider API.

The agent had no error handling for tool rate limits — it kept re-requesting the same data, looping until it hit its own max-iteration guard, and then synthesized an answer from empty results. The trace turned a two-day debugging exercise into a 20-minute root-cause analysis.
The fix was two lines: catch the rate-limit error in the tool span and set the span status to
, plus add a circuit-breaker that stops the loop after three consecutive tool failures on the same tool name.
In Python asyncio, context variables are not automatically inherited by tasks created with
. You must explicitly capture and restore the context:
Forgetting to end spans on error.
calls. Unclosed spans never reach the backend.
Storing full prompt text in span attributes.

OTel attribute values are strings with a default 128-character limit in many backends. Storing a 4,000-token prompt in an attribute either gets truncated silently or inflates storage costs. Store a SHA-256 hash of the prompt and log the full text separately.

A 1% head-based sample rate on a high-volume agent cluster means most failed traces are dropped before they reach the backend. Use tail-based sampling with a rule that always keeps traces containing error spans.
Not setting service.name in the resource.
all spans appear under "unknown_service" and the service map in the UI is useless.

Span overhead is roughly 1–5 microseconds of CPU plus a few hundred bytes of memory. Creating thousands of spans per second in a tight loop will measurably slow the agent and flood the backend. Use span events or attributes for sub-step detail instead of child spans.
at the entry point and propagate it as a span attribute throughout the entire trace. This makes it easy to filter all spans for one task across agents.
when catching an exception inside a span — this makes the waterfall red in the UI and triggers alerts.
Use the OTel Collector as an intermediate hop. Export from the agent to the collector over
. The collector handles batching, retry, and backend fan-out without slowing the agent.

Apply tail-based sampling at the collector for agent workloads. Configure it to always retain traces with error spans, traces longer than 30 seconds, or traces with more than 50 spans (potential runaway loops).
to every LLM span. This enables cost dashboards and per-task cost attribution with no additional instrumentation.
Define a canonical attribute schema for your team and document it. Inconsistent attribute names across agents (e.g.,

is the correct processor for production. It queues spans in memory and exports them in batches asynchronously, so the agent loop never blocks on network I/O. The default batch size is 512 spans and the default export interval is 5 seconds — both are configurable.
For very high-volume agent fleets (thousands of concurrent tasks), consider:
— only write 5–10% of successful, short traces to storage. Always write error traces and slow traces (p99 latency outliers).
— do not use user IDs or dynamic strings as attribute keys. High-cardinality keys inflate backend index sizes.
— deploy multiple OTel Collector replicas behind a load balancer. The collector is stateless for span pipeline use cases.
— Jaeger with Elasticsearch or Cassandra backend scales to hundreds of millions of spans per day; Honeycomb uses columnar storage designed for high-cardinality trace queries.
Vendor-neutral; supports traces, metrics, logs in one SDK
CNCF project; excellent waterfall UI; runs in Docker
Simpler than Jaeger; limited query language
High-cardinality columnar queries; strong for agent debugging
Integrated with Datadog metrics and logs; LLM-specific dashboards
Native integration with Lambda, ECS, Bedrock; OTel-compatible
LangChain native; shows chain execution, token counts, costs
Open-source; OTel-based; designed for LLM traces with eval support
Distributed tracing and structured logging complement each other: logs provide the verbose prose inside a service; traces provide the timing and correlation graph across services. Run both. Use
as fields in every log line so logs and traces are cross-linked in the backend.
Never write user-submitted content, API keys, or PII into span attributes. Use a sanitizer that hashes or truncates sensitive fields before they reach the OTel SDK.
The OTel Collector gRPC endpoint (port 4317) should be accessible only from within the cluster network. Expose it to the agent pods via a Kubernetes
SaaS backends (Honeycomb, Datadog) require an API key. Store it in the collector configuration, not in the agent process, so rotating the key requires only a collector restart.
retries failed exports up to a configurable limit. Set
to values that match expected span rates to avoid in-memory queue overflow.
Head-based sampling decisions must be propagated via
so all downstream spans follow the same sampling decision. Do not make independent sampling decisions per span — it produces inconsistent partial traces.

Think of a trace as a nested call stack, but one that persists after the program finishes and can span multiple processes. Each frame in the stack is a span. When you call a function it pushes a frame; when the function returns it pops. When the agent calls a tool over HTTP, the tool microservice gets its own stack, but that stack is stitched onto the parent stack via the trace ID — just as if it were a function call in the same process.

header is the mechanism that passes the "return address" — the parent span ID — across the network boundary. Without it the remote stack is disconnected, floating orphan spans that no trace viewer can assemble into a coherent picture.
For agents specifically: the reasoning loop is a
. Each iteration is a new set of nested spans but they all share the same
that was assigned when the user request arrived. The step number attribute is what lets you scroll through the iterations in order.
Distributed tracing connects every span in an agent task — LLM calls, tool calls, retrieval — into one queryable end-to-end graph via a shared
Agent tracing is harder than microservice tracing because execution depth, loop count, and tool call order are non-deterministic.
for production — it is vendor-neutral and incurs near-zero latency overhead on the agent.
headers on every outbound HTTP tool call using
Apply tail-based sampling at the OTel Collector to always keep error traces and loop-explosion traces, while reducing storage costs for normal successful traces.
Never redact a PII field by omitting it — audit every attribute name against your data governance policy before deploying instrumentation to production.
